# JavaScript Best Practices — AI Agent Rules File
# Generated by JS Sensei · jssensei.dev

## HOW TO USE THIS FILE

Drop this file into your project to make AI coding agents follow professional
JavaScript best practices. Supports:

| Agent | Location |
|-------|----------|
| **Claude Code** | Rename to `CLAUDE.md` at project root |
| **Cursor** | Save to `.cursor/rules/js-best-practices.mdc` |
| **GitHub Copilot** | Save to `.github/copilot-instructions.md` |
| **VS Code Copilot** | Save to `.github/copilot-instructions.md` |

Once in place, AI agents will follow all rules below when generating or
modifying JavaScript/TypeScript code in this project.

---

## SCOPE

These rules apply to ALL JavaScript and TypeScript code generated or modified
in this project. Enforce them strictly. If you see existing code that violates
these rules, fix it in the same edit if the scope is small, or flag it with
a comment.

---

## CATEGORY 1: NAMING & READABILITY

### Rule 1 — Use descriptive, meaningful variable names

NEVER use single letters (except `i`, `j`, `k` as loop indices), cryptic
abbreviations, or placeholder names like `data`, `temp`, `foo`, `x`, `res`.

```js
// WRONG
const d = new Date();
const x = users.filter(u => u.a > 18);

// CORRECT
const currentDate = new Date();
const adultUsers = users.filter(user => user.age > 18);
```

### Rule 2 — Follow JavaScript naming conventions consistently

- **Variables and functions** → `camelCase`
- **Classes and React components** → `PascalCase`
- **Top-level immutable constants** → `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE`
- **Private class fields** → `#privateField` (modern) or `_privateField` (legacy)
- **Boolean variables** → prefix with `is`, `has`, `can`, `should`

```js
// CORRECT
const MAX_RETRIES = 3;
let isLoading = false;
function fetchUserProfile() {}
class UserService {}
```

### Rule 3 — Never use magic numbers or magic strings

Extract all literal values that carry meaning into named constants defined
at the top of the module. Numeric separators (`_`) improve readability for
large numbers.

```js
// WRONG
setTimeout(fn, 86400000);
if (status === 3) { }

// CORRECT
const ONE_DAY_MS = 86_400_000;
const STATUS_PENDING = 3;
setTimeout(fn, ONE_DAY_MS);
if (status === STATUS_PENDING) { }
```

### Rule 4 — Keep functions small and single-purpose

Each function must do one thing. If you need a comment explaining WHAT the
function does (not WHY), it's too large — split it. Target: functions that
fit on one screen (~20 lines max, exceptions for configuration objects).

### Rule 5 — Write self-documenting code; use comments to explain WHY

Good naming is preferred over comments. When you do comment, explain WHY
something is done that way, not WHAT the code does.

```js
// WRONG: comment explains what the code already says
// Loop through users and check if active
for (const user of users) {
  if (user.isActive) result.push(user);
}

// CORRECT: comment explains the non-obvious reason
// Filter to only active users — inactive users should not appear
// in the digest even if they have unread notifications
const eligibleUsers = users.filter(user => user.isActive);
```

---

## CATEGORY 2: VARIABLES & DECLARATIONS

### Rule 6 — Use const by default; use let only when reassignment is needed; never use var

`var` is BANNED. Default to `const`. Only use `let` when you know the
binding will be reassigned.

```js
// WRONG
var userId = 123;
var counter = 0;

// CORRECT
const userId = 123;
let counter = 0; // will be incremented
```

### Rule 7 — Declare variables as close as possible to where they are used

Do NOT hoist all declarations to the top of a function. Declare at the
point of first use. This improves readability and narrows scope.

### Rule 8 — Avoid polluting the global scope

Never declare variables at the global level. Use modules (`export`/`import`),
block scope (`{}`), or `const`/`let` at the narrowest scope possible.

### Rule 9 — Use destructuring for multi-property access

When accessing two or more properties from the same object or array,
use destructuring.

```js
// WRONG
const name = user.name;
const email = user.email;
const role = user.role;

// CORRECT
const { name, email, role } = user;
const [first, second, ...rest] = items;
```

---

## CATEGORY 3: FUNCTIONS

### Rule 10 — Use default parameters, not || fallbacks

Default parameters are clearer AND correctly handle cases where `0`, `""`,
or `false` are valid intentional values (which `||` would override).

```js
// WRONG — replaces "" and 0 with default (often a bug)
function greet(name) {
  name = name || "World";
}

// CORRECT
function greet(name = "World") { }
```

### Rule 11 — Use early returns (guard clauses) to reduce nesting

Validate and exit early rather than wrapping all logic in nested if blocks.
Maximum nesting depth: 3 levels.

```js
// WRONG — pyramid of doom
function process(order) {
  if (order) {
    if (order.items.length > 0) {
      if (order.user.isVerified) {
        return charge(order);
      }
    }
  }
}

// CORRECT — guard clauses
function process(order) {
  if (!order) return;
  if (order.items.length === 0) return;
  if (!order.user.isVerified) return;
  return charge(order);
}
```

### Rule 12 — Prefer pure functions; avoid hidden side effects

Functions should not modify external state, mutate their arguments, or
produce different results for the same inputs. Isolate side effects to
clearly named functions.

### Rule 13 — Use arrow functions for inline callbacks

Arrow functions are shorter and preserve `this` from the surrounding
lexical scope (critical in class methods).

```js
// WRONG
const doubled = numbers.map(function(n) { return n * 2; });

// CORRECT
const doubled = numbers.map(n => n * 2);
```

---

## CATEGORY 4: ERROR HANDLING

### Rule 14 — Always handle promise rejections — never leave them unhandled

Every `async` function that calls other async operations must handle
rejection via `try/catch` or `.catch()`. Unhandled rejections crash in
Node.js and silently fail in browsers.

```js
// WRONG
async function loadUser(id) {
  const user = await getUser(id); // if this throws, error is lost
  return user;
}

// CORRECT
async function loadUser(id) {
  try {
    return await getUser(id);
  } catch (err) {
    logger.error("loadUser failed:", err);
    throw err; // re-throw so callers know it failed
  }
}
```

### Rule 15 — Throw Error instances with descriptive messages, never strings

`throw "error"` produces no stack trace. Always `throw new Error("message")`.
Use specific error types (`TypeError`, `RangeError`) when appropriate.

```js
// WRONG
throw "invalid input";
throw { code: 400 };

// CORRECT
throw new Error("User ID is required");
throw new TypeError(`Expected string id, got ${typeof id}`);
```

### Rule 16 — Wrap only the risky operation in try/catch, not entire functions

Narrow try/catch blocks make it clear exactly which operation can fail
and produce better error messages.

---

## CATEGORY 5: ASYNC & PROMISES

### Rule 17 — Prefer async/await over .then() chains

`async/await` code is more readable, easier to debug, and has better
stack traces. Use `.then()` only when you genuinely need to chain without
`await` (rare).

### Rule 18 — Use Promise.all for independent parallel async operations

Sequential `await` in a loop is a performance bug when the operations
are independent. Always use `Promise.all` for concurrent independent calls.

```js
// WRONG — 600ms total (sequential)
const user = await getUser(id);
const posts = await getPosts(id);
const friends = await getFriends(id);

// CORRECT — ~200ms total (parallel)
const [user, posts, friends] = await Promise.all([
  getUser(id),
  getPosts(id),
  getFriends(id),
]);
```

### Rule 19 — Never mix async patterns (callbacks + promises + async/await)

Choose ONE pattern for a codebase and stick to it. Preferred order:
1. async/await (primary)
2. Promises with .then/.catch (acceptable)
3. Callbacks (only for legacy APIs, wrap them in promisify)

---

## CATEGORY 6: ARRAYS & OBJECTS

### Rule 20 — Use array methods over imperative for loops

`.map()`, `.filter()`, `.find()`, `.some()`, `.every()`, `.reduce()` are
more expressive and composable than manual for loops. Use for...of only
when you need `break`/`continue` or the index.

```js
// WRONG
const prices = [];
for (let i = 0; i < products.length; i++) {
  prices.push(products[i].price);
}

// CORRECT
const prices = products.map(product => product.price);
```

### Rule 21 — Never mutate function arguments or external state

Always return new values; never modify objects or arrays that were passed in.
Use spread for shallow clones, `structuredClone()` for deep clones.

```js
// WRONG — mutates the caller's array
function addId(users) {
  users.forEach((user, i) => { user.id = i; });
}

// CORRECT — returns new array
function addId(users) {
  return users.map((user, i) => ({ ...user, id: i }));
}
```

### Rule 22 — Use optional chaining (?.) for deep property access

Never write `obj && obj.a && obj.a.b`. Use `obj?.a?.b` which returns
`undefined` safely if any part is `null` or `undefined`.

```js
// WRONG
const city = user && user.address && user.address.city;

// CORRECT
const city = user?.address?.city;
const name = obj?.getName?.();   // safe method call
const tag = article?.tags?.[0]; // safe array access
```

---

## CATEGORY 7: CODE QUALITY & PATTERNS

### Rule 23 — Always use strict equality (===) and strict inequality (!==)

NEVER use `==` or `!=` except for the one valid use case: checking for
both `null` and `undefined` simultaneously with `value != null`.

```js
// WRONG
if (userId == "123") { }  // coerces types — unpredictable
if (0 == false) { }       // true — unintended coercion

// CORRECT
if (userId === "123") { }
if (value != null) { }    // intentional: catches null AND undefined
```

### Rule 24 — Use template literals for string construction

Template literals (`\`...\``) are required for any string involving variables
or expressions. Never use `+` concatenation for string building.

```js
// WRONG
const msg = "Hello, " + name + "! You have " + count + " items.";

// CORRECT
const msg = `Hello, ${name}! You have ${count} items.`;
```

### Rule 25 — Keep files and modules focused (Single Responsibility)

One concern per file. If a file is growing large or has unrelated exports,
split it. Target: files under 300 lines. Name files after their primary export.

```
// WRONG: utils.js with 500 lines mixing unrelated utilities

// CORRECT:
// formatters.js  — only formatting functions
// validators.js  — only validation functions
// auth.js        — only authentication logic
```

### Rule 26 — Ensure ESLint and Prettier are configured and passing

All generated code must pass ESLint with at minimum these rules enabled:
- `no-var: "error"`
- `prefer-const: "error"`
- `eqeqeq: "error"`
- `no-unused-vars: "warn"`

Prettier settings (if present in project, respect them; otherwise default):
- `semi: true`
- `singleQuote: true`
- `tabWidth: 2`
- `trailingComma: "es5"`

---

## QUICK REFERENCE CHEAT SHEET

| Topic | WRONG | CORRECT |
|-------|-------|---------|
| Variables | `var x = 1` | `const x = 1` |
| Equality | `x == 1` | `x === 1` |
| Strings | `"Hi " + name` | `` `Hi ${name}` `` |
| Defaults | `name \|\| "World"` | `name = "World"` |
| Null check | `x && x.y && x.y.z` | `x?.y?.z` |
| Parallel async | `await` in loop | `Promise.all([...])` |
| Mutation | `arr.push(x)` in function | `return [...arr, x]` |
| Errors | `throw "error"` | `throw new Error("msg")` |
| Loops | `for` + `push` | `.map()` / `.filter()` |
| Nesting | Pyramid of ifs | Guard clause + early return |
